behaviors.description: The speech-to-text output preserves speaker labels so that the summary can attribute statements to specific attendees.
behaviors.justification: A mock speech-to-text API can return any response, including perfect speaker labels. The real API might not support speaker diarization or might format it differently (or not at all). The real failure boundary is whether the actual external API preserves speaker labels — this is hidden by mocks. Only a System-level test calling the real speech-to-text API reveals whether speaker labels are actually returned in a format the downstream summarizer can parse.
behaviors.test_description: Call a real speech-to-text service with a multi-speaker audio file (at least 2 distinct speakers with clear, overlapping contributions). Verify the returned transcript includes speaker labels (e.g., 'Speaker 1:', 'Speaker 2:' or diarization markers). Parse the response to confirm labels are consistent, parseable, and correctly attribute each statement. Verify that merging this output into the LLM prompt preserves speaker attribution downstream.
behaviors.plan_consistent_note: Integration test with mocked API would always pass with perfect speaker labels. Real failure is API behavior. System-level required.

behaviors.description: The structured summary contains at least one action item for every attendee who was assigned a task during the meeting.
behaviors.justification: Whether the LLM correctly extracts and attributes action items to all assigned attendees depends entirely on the real LLM's understanding of meeting context and task assignment patterns. A mocked LLM can be instructed to return action items for every attendee, hiding the real failure: the production LLM might miss implicit assignments, misattribute tasks, or conflate them with other statements. Only a real LLM call with a complex, realistic meeting transcript where attendees are assigned multiple distinct tasks reveals whether this invariant holds consistently.
behaviors.test_description: Call the real LLM with a meeting transcript where at least 3 different attendees are explicitly assigned distinct tasks using varied language patterns (e.g., 'Alice will draft the proposal by Friday', 'Bob, can you schedule a follow-up?', 'Carol, please send the document'). Verify the structured summary contains at least one action item for each of these 3 attendees in the 'action_items' array. Confirm attributions match assigned individuals and no tasks are missed or duplicated.
behaviors.plan_consistent_note: Mocked LLM always returns expected action items. Real LLM might miss implicit assignments or misattribute. Cannot mock LLM's comprehension. Agentic-level required.

behaviors.description: Key decisions mentioned in the transcript appear in the 'decisions' section of the summary, not buried in the narrative.
behaviors.justification: The LLM must both identify what constitutes a 'decision' in meeting context (vs. deliberation, question, or observation) and correctly route it to the decisions field. A mocked LLM always structures data correctly. A real LLM might conflate decisions with narrative content, forget to extract them, or place them in the wrong field. Only calling the real LLM on a complex transcript with multiple decision types (strategic, technical, procedural) and mixed narrative reveals whether the LLM correctly distinguishes and structures decisions.
behaviors.test_description: Call the real LLM with a meeting transcript containing 4-5 explicit decision statements embedded in conversation (e.g., 'We decided to postpone the launch to Q2', 'The team chose React over Vue for performance', 'We agreed to meet weekly on Mondays'). Verify the structured summary has a non-empty 'decisions' array containing all mentioned decisions. Confirm decisions do not appear *only* in the narrative field. Validate decision content is accurate and not paraphrased incorrectly or duplicated.
behaviors.plan_consistent_note: Mocked LLM always populates decisions field correctly. Real LLM might misclassify or miss decisions. Real LLM comprehension needed. Agentic-level required.

behaviors.description: When the audio file is longer than 60 minutes, the service splits it into segments and processes them sequentially, merging the results into a single coherent summary.
behaviors.justification: The real failure modes are in the segmentation and merging layer: cutting audio mid-sentence, losing context at boundaries, creating duplicate content, or producing incoherent merged output. These require real audio file handling and integration with the actual speech-to-text pipeline. A unit test cannot verify end-to-end coherence; a mock audio file cannot expose real segmentation bugs. The LLM-based summarization of merged segments (if used) can be mocked for this behavior's core test. System-level complexity (subprocess, OS-specific audio handling) is secondary to the primary failure boundary.
behaviors.test_description: Upload a real or realistic synthesized audio file longer than 60 minutes (multi-speaker, diverse meeting content). Verify the service correctly segments it (e.g., 10-minute chunks) without cutting off speakers or losing audio. Confirm the merged transcript preserves all content, with no duplicated segments or gaps. Validate that the final structured summary covers the full meeting scope (all attendees, decisions, and action items from all segments appear). Re-test with a 90-minute file to ensure scaling.
behaviors.plan_consistent_note: Test requires real audio handling and integration with actual speech-to-text. Mocked LLM sufficient for merging verification. Subprocess/System complexity is secondary. Integration-level is minimum.

behaviors.description: The full pipeline — upload audio, transcribe, summarize, deliver structured output — completes without manual intervention for a real meeting recording.
behaviors.justification: This is an end-to-end, multi-step agentic pipeline. Real LLM behavior at each stage (handling speaker-labeled transcripts, extracting action items, identifying decisions) affects whether the pipeline completes successfully. Real external API calls (speech-to-text, LLM summarization) introduce non-determinism and potential failures (timeouts, format mismatches, rate limits). Real infrastructure (file storage, async coordination, retries) matters. A System-level test of individual components or an Agentic test of a single LLM call cannot reveal whether all steps orchestrate correctly. Only a Workflow-level test executing the complete pipeline start-to-end with a real meeting recording reveals if the full system succeeds without manual intervention.
behaviors.test_description: Execute the complete pipeline with a real, diverse meeting recording (30-90 minutes, multiple speakers, varied content including decisions and task assignments): (1) upload audio file, (2) trigger transcription, (3) submit transcript for summarization, (4) retrieve structured JSON output with 'action_items', 'decisions', 'narrative', and speaker 'attendees' fields. Verify all fields are populated with non-empty arrays/strings, JSON is valid, no step fails with an error, and the output correctly reflects meeting content. Run the test 3+ times to confirm non-deterministic LLM behavior doesn't cause intermittent pipeline failures.
behaviors.plan_consistent_note: Requires all real APIs (speech-to-text, LLM), real file handling, network latency, and LLM non-determinism across multiple steps. Cannot be effectively mocked without missing real integration and orchestration failures. Workflow-level is minimum.
